# methods
## snc
16062.1.2.16063.1

please cf. @schwarz_this_2026 for the corpus building and evaluation script (still messy.)

## data

```{r,m1}
#| echo: false
#| output: false

plist3<-list()
#ts<-plist$ts
#ts
lmdf.c<-plist$df_agg
mpr<-!lmdf.c$post
mpo<-lmdf.c$post
mg<-lmdf.c$target=="gpt"

shpr<-sum(lmdf.c$n[c(which(mpr))])
shpo<-sum(lmdf.c$n[c(which(mpo))])
sg<-sum(lmdf.c$n[c(which(mg))])
ts<-data.frame(target=c("gemini","human-pre","human-post"),tokens=c(sg,shpr,shpo))
plist3$ts<-ts
```

our human langugae data consists of raw texts from german bundestag plenary protocols (@dip_dip_2026). the LLM corpus consists of  model summaries of a first subset of these texts generated with the following prompt: @sec-gemini-prompt.

### corpus subsets
`r knitr::kable(ts)`

### gemini prompt {#sec-gemini-prompt}
```{r,prompt}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| eval: true

ptx<-readLines("../g-prompt01.txt")
print(ptx)
plist3$ptx<-ptx
```

```{r,prompt-dep}
#| results: asis
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| eval: false

ptx<-readLines("../g-prompt01.txt")
cat("```text\n",
    paste(ptx, collapse = "\n"),
    "\n```",
    sep = "")
```

## computation
we first devised AI-typical lemmata in the model corpus which are distinct for that corpus by log frequency calculation, see @fig-gpt.dplot

```{r, prelim}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
#| eval: true

lmdf.c<-plist$df_agg
#dfs$gp <- lemma_pref$log_odds[match(dfs$lemma,lemma_pref$lemma)]
lmdf.c$in.gp <- lmdf.c$lemma%in%lmdf.c$lemma[mg]

mh1<-lmdf.c$in.gp&lmdf.c$target=="human"
#sum(mh1)
sh1<-sum(lmdf.c$n[mh1])/sum(lmdf.c$n[lmdf.c$target=="human"])
mp1<-lmdf.c$in.gp&lmdf.c$post
#sum(mp1,na.rm = T)
sp1<-sum(lmdf.c$n[mp1],na.rm=T)/sum(lmdf.c$n[lmdf.c$post],na.rm = T)
mg1<-lmdf.c$in.gp&lmdf.c$target=="gpt"
sg1<-sum(lmdf.c$n[mg1])/sum(lmdf.c$n[lmdf.c$target=="gpt"])
sp1>sh1 # TRUE!
### > more occurences (rel. frequencies) of gpt vocabular in post gpt corpus
sp1-sh1 # only 0.02587 points, #16143. > 0.003001367
p.df<-lmdf.c
p.df$target[p.df$target=="0-human"]<-"human"
s.df<-data.frame(target=c("pre","post","DIFF:"),freq=c(sh1,sp1,sp1-sh1))
s.df$freq<-round(s.df$freq,4)
#s.df
plist3$sdf<-s.df
plist3$pdf<-p.df
```


```{r,gptplot}
#| echo: false
#| output: true
#| label: fig-gpt.dplot
#| fig-cap: "lemma gpt scores over targets"

# plot(p.df$in.gp, p.df$rel_freq, 
#      col  = as.factor(p.df$target), 
#      pch  = 19, 
#      xlab = "in.gp (GPT typicality score)",
#      ylab = "f.rel (relative frequency)",
#      main = "GPT scores vs relative frequency by target"

#      )
# legend("topright", 
#             legend = levels(as.factor(p.df$target)),
#             col   = 1:length(levels(as.factor(p.df$target))), 
#             pch   = 19)
# plist3$gptplot<-recordPlot()
plist$gptplot2
#gpt.plot
```


```{r,gplot}
#| echo: false
#| output: true
#| label: fig-densplot01
#| fig-cap: "target gpt density"
#| eval: false


library(ggplot2)
dff2<-p.df[c(which(mh1),which(mp1),which(mg1)),]
gplot<-ggplot(data = dff2, aes(x = f.rel, fill = target)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.3) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 0.12)) +  # set your range here
  labs(title = "Density Plot", x = "GPT score", y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal()
plist$dff2<-dff2
plist$gplot<-gplot
gplot
```



# evaluation
## basic descriptive
to first gather an insight, yet with simple descriptive stats comparing the raw frequencies of gpt-preferred lemmas in pre- and post-gemini onset we find that in the target corpus the occurences of these lemma increase, only by small amount (see @tbl-s.df) and hard to visualise (see @fig-boxplot01). wether these findings become relevant, we'll see in @sec-lm-1 where we evaluate the frequencies with a linear regression model.

```{r,kablediff}
#| echo: false
#| output: true
#| label: tbl-s.df
#| tbl-cap: "GPT lemma frequencies (table) over target. (freq / vH)"
#| warning: false

knitr::kable(s.df,label = "tab-s.df")
```


```{r,boxdesc}
#| echo: false
#| output: true
#| label: fig-boxplot01
#| fig-cap: "GPT lemma frequencies (boxplot) over target. (freq / vH)"
#| warning: false


# boxplot(f.rel~target,p.df[c(which(mh1),which(mp1),which(mg1)),],outline=F,notch=T,
# boxplot(f.rel~target,p.df[c(which(mh1),which(mp1)),],outline=F,notch=T,
# b1<-boxplot(rel_freq~post,p.df[c(which(mh1),which(mp1)),],outline=F,notch=T,
# main="descriptive stats: GPT vocab raw frequencies")
plist$boxdesc2
#b1$stats[3,]
#b1$stats[3,2]-b1$stats[3,1]
#plist3$boxdesc<-recordPlot()
b1<-plist$b1
lb0<-plist$lb0
lb1<-length(b1)
```

## responsible lemmata
The `r lb1` lemma where 1. the relative frequency of lemma in target=post is higher than in target=pre (n=`r lb0`) and 2. that frequency exceeds the mean relative frequency is displayed in @fig-barplot.

```{r,eval=T}
#| fig-cap: "lemma frequency increase of relevant AI typical lemma"
#| label: "fig-barplot"
#| echo: FALSE
#| warning: FALSE

# knitr::include_graphics("../../../q/germanic/001/gemini_files/figure-html/fig-densplot01-1.png")

plist$barplot
# b1<-plist$b1
# lb0<-plist$lb0
# lb1<-length(b1)
```

### lemma descriptive output
```{r,lemma-res-desc}
#| echo: false
#| output: true
#| warning: false
#| eval: false

load("../lemma-resp.tj3.RData")

head(tj3$WORD[order(tj3$diff,decreasing = T)],20)
plist$
```

### lemma linear model output
```{r,lemma-res-lm}
#| echo: false
#| output: true
#| warning: false
#| eval: false

load("../lemma-res.lres.RData")
lres.h<-head(lres,20)
lres.h$lemma
```


## linear regression {#sec-lm-1}
to prove descriptive results, we compute the stability of the frequency increase for target- vs. reference corpus with a linear regression model using R's lme4::lmer() function, cf. @bates_fitting_2015. coefficents are printed below, where `freq_pmw` is the lemma frequencies/pmw over corpus; `post` defines reference resp. target corpus [post==TRUE|FALSE] (pre/post) and gp as numerical variable representing the gpt-score of the corresponding lemma i.e. wether it scores high (positive values) or low (negative values) in terms of being preferredly used by the chat agent.

```{r,lm-models}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
#| warning: false

library(lme4)
library(lmerTest)
df_lg.norm<-plist$df_lg.norm
# lm1<-lm(f.rel~target*in.gp,lmdf.c)
# lm2<-lmer(f.rel~target*in.gp+(1|lemma),lmdf.c)
lm1<-lmer(freq_pmw~post+gp+(1|lemma),df_lg.norm)
#summary(lm1)
lm2<-lm(freq_pmw~post+gp,df_lg.norm)
#summary(lm2)

```

### basic (lm)
formula: `frequency.pmw ~ post + gpt.score`

```{r,lm}
#| echo: false
#| output: true
#| warning: false

summary(lm2)

```

### random effects model (lmer)
formula: `frequency.pmw ~ post + gpt.score + (1|lemma)`

```{r,lmer}
#| echo: false
#| output: true
#| warning: false

s2<-summary(lm1)
s2
ingpvar<-s2$vcov@factors$correlation
ingpvar
ingpv<-ingpvar[,3]
ingpv<-ingpv[3]
#ingpv
pc8<-round(s2$coefficients[3,1],6)
pc1<-round(s2$coefficients[1,1],6)
#pc1<-s2$coefficients[1,1]
pcp<-round(pc8/pc1*100,2)
pc8<-round(s2$coefficients[3,1],6)
pc1<-round(s2$coefficients[1,1],6)
pcd<-pc8
pcd<-round(pcd,5)
#pcd
p100<-pc1*100
pcdp<-(pcd/p100)
#pcdp
pcdp<-round(pcdp,5)
pcd<-pc8
p100<-100/pc1
pcdp<-(pcd*p100)
#pcdp
pcdp<-round(pcdp,5)
#pcdp

#pcdp
#pcdp
#pc1<-s2$coefficients[1,1]
pcp<-round(pc8/pc1*100,2)
plist3$s2<-s2
save(plist3,file="plist4.RData")

```

#### helper interpretation, to be tested
the coefficients interesting for us are the gp and post estimates. here we test the association between the gpt score of a lemma and its estimated frequency and its showing that a general increase of frequency is estimated if the score rises (=the lemma is within the lemmas preferred used by gemini) and that for the post-gpt corpus this increase  (``` `r pcdp`% ```) is significant for single lemmata (and not random in data).

in the fixed effects correlation output of the lmer() model we see that the gpt score correlates with the target corpus frequency for lemma by ``` `r ingpv` ```.

### anova of mixed effects model [out]
```{r,anova}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
#| warning: false

print(anova(lm1))
```

# references {#references}
